# Transcrição: 4-Ad Copy & Ad Creative

- **Fonte:** 4-Ad Copy & Ad Creative
- **Duração:** 25:02
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

## [00:00 - 03:01]

But again, before I dive in, I want to take a minute to talk about LinkedIn ads compared to what many consider the gold standard of social media ads, Facebook. Many of the same ad concepts that will work for your Facebook ads, they do translate to LinkedIn. So if you have a funnel or an ad campaign that's just crushing it on Facebook, it'd be silly not to start that and test that on LinkedIn with maybe a few tweaks. And because there are a few key differences between Facebook and LinkedIn ads and that will really help you have similar success if you are having success on Facebook on LinkedIn. So let's dive in and take a look. There are four elements of a LinkedIn ad that you're going to be writing, writing or designing. First is the introductory text. That is the main body copy of your ad and we're going to go through this in more detail in a second. Your image or video, so that's just what's going to catch some of these attention as they're scrolling down the page. Your ad headline, that's just the big copy below it. And then your call to action button. So there are a couple of different options that are built in to LinkedIn. All right, so before I dive into more detail on that, I just want to let you know that there are four different types of ads that you can run on LinkedIn. There are text ads, there are dynamic ads, sponsored content, which is what you're seeing on the screen and then sponsored email. For the sake of keeping things simple and the best place to start, we're going to only focus on sponsored content and sponsored email. Reason being, that's where you're going to get the most results and that's where you're going to really want to start. Everything that we teach for these different ad placements is translate perfectly to the other placements. So just know that when you're going in, if you do run a text ad, which is just the little text bar at the top of the screen and there's some, it's kind of like a right sidebar ad. Or a dynamic ad, which is what you'll see, it's like, almost looks like a banner ad with a picture of your face on LinkedIn. A lot of the copy elements and design translate almost exactly. So just know that we're going to talk about sponsored content, which is effectively a news feed ad and then sponsored email, which is effectively sending somebody a message as an ad, a personal message inside of LinkedIn.

## [03:01 - 06:03]

So first you have the introductory text that's at the top. It's the body copy of your ad and this is where you provide the needed context around your offer, your lead magnet, really whatever you're promoting. So your prospects know exactly what they're getting whenever they opt in. Next is your video or image and the whole purpose, the sole purpose of this is to catch their attention. You really want them, nobody is on LinkedIn looking for ads to look at. Nobody on LinkedIn is looking for somebody to buy something from. So they're scrolling through for other reasons. You need to inject yourself in their life in a way that's going to interrupt what they're doing in a positive way where it doesn't look annoying or spammy or over the top marketing. So that's AdLine. Then below that is the ad headline right here. And that's where a benefit statements or calling out your prospects, whoever your audience who you're targeting and really hammering on what they're going to gain from what they get the ad. Are they going to get a lead magnet? If so, what is the benefit they're going to get out of it? And then lastly is your call to action. So the one on this ad here is this is for a direct to appointment ad. So there are five principles for conversion focused copy. So if you're going to have an ad, you want it to convert, you want people to opt in, you want people to buy something, you're asking them to do something and your copy needs to be done in a certain way that is effective and they know exactly what they're getting. So the first one is keep it short. I'm going to go through this in depth right after this slide. So I'm just going to rip through the five key principles and then we'll dive into them deeper. Principle number one is keep it short. Number two is to call out your target or your audience. Number three is to focus on why instead of focusing on what? Number four is don't get cute. And number five is include multiple call to actions. And so let's jump into that. So principle one, keep it short. We found that shorter updates, meaning 150 characters or fewer, that's not an exact metric if you want to go higher than that, that works too. But around that mark, they tend to perform best. So within a short update, you want to focus on how your target audience would benefit from clicking on the link. What are they going to really get from that? What are they going to really going to get from that ad? And that's basically it. And another reason for this is that 80% of sponsored content clicks come from mobile devices. So the shorter your ad is, the better it's going to look on mobile. It's a much smaller screen. So you're going to want to make sure that your ad and your copy looks great on small screens. You're going to be able to see a preview of it. So you'll see it just fine. Just know that's another reason why you want to keep it short. Principle number two is to call out your target or call your audience. So this ad right here, this ad right here is for people that are targeting marketing directors. and you scroll down, there's a reason that it caught my attention because I am a director of marketing. You can see that they have this on here. And then the third principle is don't get cute. LinkedIn is pretty pricey for brand awareness. I mean, chances are you're not running ads for Doritos or Apple or Bud Light on LinkedIn or IBM with a $30 million ad budget to spend on brand awareness. You or your clients or whoever you're running ads for, you need results and you probably need them pretty quickly to justify the ad spend. And since LinkedIn is a professional network, catchy taglines and cute sayings are not needed. You don't need to have... The creative, it doesn't need to seem like a Super Bowl commercial. They told us to do it. And we have weekly meetings with LinkedIn. And it's bumped up our click through rate fairly significantly. All right. So here are a few other considerations whenever you're writing your ads. I mentioned how long it should be until they see results or how long until desired action. You're going to be running video ads. That content matters a lot more than video length. So one of the most common questions we get is how long should my video be? Should it be 15 seconds? Should it be five minutes? Should it be two minutes? The real answer that it doesn't matter. Conventional wisdom says the more you're asking of them, the longer your video should be. So if you're asking them to opt in for a free e-book, the video can be very short. If you're asking them to sign up for a webinar and attend it, it can be slightly longer. If you're asking them to buy a $2,000 product or to book a sales call, it should be longer than that. But LinkedIn has found that it's not really the case. So test different links of video. I would start shorter. I would start with the conventional wisdom of the smaller the ask, the shorter the video is just to make it easier on you. But if you have a five minute long video that you want to test, I wouldn't feel bad using it. Also, content matters more than production quality. This video that you see right now is really highly produced, but it doesn't need to be that for an ad. If you have a cell phone, if you have a friend to hold it or a tripod, you just want to stand in front of the camera and talk about your offer and talk about the benefits and make an ad, that works perfectly fine. You don't need to have a production company. You don't need to have a $30,000 budget to create these ads. If you have a phone, you can create them. And also ad subtitles. ## [15:08 - 18:11]

Newsfeed ads are muted to begin with. So when somebody scrolls past it, it's not just going to blare audio at them. That's jarring and it won't work well anyway. So they're muted. Not everyone's going to click the sound button to start the sound. So get subtitles. You can go to rev.com for that, rev.com, and create subtitles for $1 a minute. But that way they can follow along with what you're saying without having to turn on the audio. Maybe they're in a public place as well and they don't want the audio to annoy people. And then again, focus on why. The video should reinforce what's in the copy. Like A, the video should stop them in the tracks to get them to want to pay more attention and read the rest of the ad. And B, it should reinforce what's also in the copy, which a big part of that is starting with why and focusing on why they should care. All right. So now if you're not going to use video and you're going to use images, let's talk about four considerations for image creative. Number one, pattern interrupt is the most important. It interrupts what they're currently doing and gets them to focus on you, your ad, your creative, your offer, your way of helping them. That is very important. Here's a good example of this. They've been kind of lax with it, but still they want you to have the least amount of text as possible. LinkedIn, there are none. There are no limitations like that.
